narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated an 80.5% chance that you would not pass, based on patterns from thousands of past students' academic and background details. This high predicted risk of failure triggered the rejection to help the school maintain strong bar passage rates. The model's decision hinged most strongly on your third-year grade decile, which ranked in the bottom tier and stood out as the biggest factor pushing toward a failure prediction—far below the mid-to-upper deciles typical among students who passed. Your first-year grade decile, also at the lowest level, played a significant supporting role in the same direction, unlike the stronger early performances seen in successful bar takers. Interestingly, your undergraduate GPA provided a slight counterbalance, nudging the prediction a bit toward passing since it was closer to the averages of those who succeeded, though not enough to offset the other concerns. Coming from an upper-middle income family and planning full-time studies aligned reasonably well with accepted students, but your LSAT score of 31 was notably lower than the 37 average for bar passers, adding to the overall risk profile. In comparison, students who got through had much higher decile rankings in their early and later undergrad years, often paired with LSATs and GPAs that signaled consistent academic strength. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's the model highlighting where your record diverges from proven paths to bar success. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your undergrad transcript through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and seeking mentorship from bar-passed alumni to build those key academic indicators. With targeted improvements, you could reapply stronger, as many students turn similar profiles around. Your full-time commitment is a great foundation—lean into it while addressing these gaps.
